_meta.purpose: DRAFT prerequisite concept vocabulary + rules (spec docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-15-prerequisite-concept-graph-design.md §1A). Pending Tybalt's exhaustive review — rename to prereq_concepts.json only after approval. Consumed by scripts/import_course_concepts.py.
_meta.method: in-session multi-agent draft: 7 discipline-family proposers over the full in-scope inventory (4,730 CC courses, 230 UC receiving) -> synthesis -> 2 adversarial verifiers (coverage, rules); review doc at docs/prereq-concepts-review.md
conventions: Lab-only UC asks (e.g. PHYSICS 4AL, CHEM 1AL) are covered by their lecture concept; labs do not get separate concepts.
Corequisites are not encoded; where a relationship is commonly concurrent, encoding it as a completed prerequisite is a per-concept normative call recorded in that concept's note.
Combined courses (e.g. 'Linear Algebra and Differential Equations') map to BOTH constituent concepts.
Courses with no concept stay unmapped by design; concepts exist only for recurring pathway courses anchored by UC asks.
_meta.review: Approved 2026-07-15 by Tybalt ('that's fine, proceed'): decisions 1-5 stand as drafted (linear_alg<-calc_3 and diff_eq<-linear_alg are his stated normative calls; consequences accepted as documented); decision 6: software-tools cluster left unmapped for v1; decision 7: intro_stats/engl_comp_2 kept as CC-side concepts; decision 8: variance notes added post-approval. Combined-course convention for classification: a course spanning multiple concepts maps to the DEEPEST constituent in the partial order, flagged 'combined_course' (single-concept schema, v1); listed in QA for manual override.

concepts.name: Calculus I (single-variable, differential)
concepts.note: The start of the STEM math track. Honors, 'with support', and early/late-transcendentals sections all count. There's no precalc or trig concept because those courses never appear in the agreements. A course that combines Calc 1 and 2 counts as both.
example_cc_titles: Single Variable Calculus I - Early Transcendentals
Calculus with Analytic Geometry, First Course

concepts.name: Calculus II (integration, series)
concepts.note: Standard second-semester calculus. Honors and support versions count too.
example_cc_titles: Single Variable Calculus II - Late Transcendentals

concepts.name: Calculus III (multivariable / vector calculus)
concepts.note: Covers all of multivariable and vector calculus in one concept. Quarter-system schools that split it into a fourth course map those here — there's no Calc 4. It also stands in for the separate vector-calculus courses a few UCs ask for.

concepts.name: Linear Algebra
concepts.note: We require Calc 3 before Linear Algebra everywhere, even though many colleges only require Calc 2 or let you take them together. It's the deliberately conservative choice. Courses that teach Linear Algebra and Differential Equations together use the combined concept instead.

concepts.name: Ordinary Differential Equations
concepts.note: We require Linear Algebra before Differential Equations so there's always a defined order — even though most colleges only ask for Calc 2 or 3, with linear algebra at most a co-requisite. Calc 3 comes through linear algebra. Combined LA+DE courses use the combined concept.

concepts.name: Discrete Mathematics / Discrete Structures
concepts.note: One concept for Discrete Math / Discrete Structures, whether the math or CS department teaches it. We require Calc 1 OR Applied/Business Calc 1 as a readiness gate — either satisfies it. Some colleges gate on precalc (out of scope) or treat calculus as a co-req, but a completed calculus course is the conservative call.

concepts.name: Applied/Business Calculus I
concepts.note: The applied/business calculus track — business, social-science, and 'calculus for biology and medicine' versions all fold in. It's a separate on-ramp: it does not count as regular Calc 1 and doesn't feed the STEM chain.
example_cc_titles: Calculus for Business and Social Sciences
Basic Techniques of Applied Calculus I
Calculus for Biology and Medicine I

concepts.name: Applied/Business Calculus II
concepts.note: Second course of the applied/business calculus track. Some colleges only offer the first course.
example_cc_titles: Calculus for Biology and Medicine II

concepts.name: Calculus-Based Probability & Statistics for STEM
concepts.note: Calculus-based probability and statistics for STEM and engineering. We require Calc 2 as the floor (colleges vary from Calc 1 up to multivariable). Plain, non-calculus intro statistics is a separate concept.

concepts.name: Introductory Statistics (algebra-based)
concepts.note: Standard algebra-based intro statistics (~110 colleges, sometimes under a PSYC or BUS prefix). No transfer-level prerequisite — the algebra gate is pre-transfer. Distinct from the calculus-based STEM statistics.

concepts.name: Calculus-Based Physics: Mechanics
concepts.note: First course of the calculus-based physics sequence for scientists and engineers. We require Calc 1 completed, though many colleges let you take it alongside. Honors versions, attached labs, and 'mechanics + waves' packagings all fold in.
example_cc_titles: Physics for Scientists and Engineers: Mechanics
Classical Mechanics for Scientists and Engineers

concepts.name: Calculus-Based Physics: Electricity & Magnetism
concepts.note: Second calculus-based physics course (electricity and magnetism). We require Calc 2. Some colleges also want Calc 3 alongside, but we don't encode that to avoid overstating the requirement.
example_cc_titles: Physics for Scientists and Engineers: Electricity and Magnetism

concepts.name: Calculus-Based Physics: Waves, Thermodynamics & Optics
concepts.note: Third calculus-based physics course (waves, thermodynamics, optics). We require E&M first to keep one consistent statewide order, even though some colleges allow it right after mechanics.
example_cc_titles: Physics for Scientists and Engineers: Fluids, Waves, Optics and Thermodynamics
Heat, Waves, Light and Modern Physics

concepts.name: Calculus-Based Physics: Modern Physics
concepts.note: A fourth physics course at schools that have one (modern physics). At three-course schools this content lives inside the waves/thermo course and nothing maps here — that gap is intentional.
example_cc_titles: Modern Physics for Scientists and Engineers
Physics for Scientists and Engineers: Modern Physics

concepts.name: General Physics I (Algebra/Trig-Based)
concepts.note: First semester of the algebra-based physics sequence for life-science majors. No prerequisite in scope (the usual precalc/trig gate isn't in our data). Lecture and its separate 1-unit lab count together.

concepts.name: General Physics II (Algebra/Trig-Based)
concepts.note: Second semester of the algebra-based physics sequence. The community-college side is two semesters, so there's no third algebra-based concept.
example_cc_titles: General Physics - Electricity, Magnetism and Optics

concepts.name: General Chemistry I
concepts.note: Start of the chemistry chain (General Chemistry 1). No math prerequisite in our model — the real gate is algebra readiness, which is pre-transfer. Lecture/lab split offerings and honors versions all count together.
example_cc_titles: General Chemistry and Chemical Analysis I

concepts.name: General Chemistry II
concepts.note: Completes the full-year general chemistry sequence. A quarter-system school's third-quarter course folds in here — there's no separate Gen Chem 3.

concepts.name: Organic Chemistry I
concepts.note: Organic Chemistry 1. We require the full year of general chemistry first — a few schools admit after one semester, but the full-year gate is the common pattern and the conservative choice.
example_cc_titles: Organic Chemistry for Science Majors I

concepts.name: Organic Chemistry II
concepts.note: Completes the organic chemistry sequence; third-quarter organic at quarter-system schools folds in here.
example_cc_titles: Organic Chemistry for Science Majors II

concepts.name: Programming Fundamentals (CS1)
concepts.note: Intro programming (CS1). Python, Java, C++, and C first courses all count, since UCs treat them interchangeably. Computer-literacy or survey courses do not. Honors versions and two-course CS1 splits fold in.
example_cc_titles: Programming Concepts and Methodology I: C++
Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs

concepts.name: Intermediate / Object-Oriented Programming (CS2)
concepts.note: The intermediate / object-oriented programming course between CS1 and data structures. The typical California sequence is three courses, and several UCs ask for a distinct OOP course before data structures. Language-agnostic.
example_cc_titles: Programming Concepts and Methodology II: C++

concepts.name: Data Structures & Algorithms (CS3)
concepts.note: Data Structures and Algorithms. We require the OOP course first everywhere, even though a minority of colleges go straight from CS1 — same conservative approach as Linear Algebra requiring Calc 3.

concepts.name: Computer Organization & Assembly Language
concepts.note: Computer Organization and Assembly Language. We require only CS1, the common sending-side prerequisite, even though several UC equivalents sit after data structures — we sequence by what community colleges actually require.

concepts.name: C / Systems Programming (second language)
concepts.note: A C or systems-programming course taken after a first programming language (four UCs ask for one). A C course that's a college's first programming course maps to CS1 instead. Requires CS1.
example_cc_titles: C as a Second Programming Language

concepts.name: Digital Logic Design
concepts.note: Digital Logic Design. UC versions straddle CS and EE departments, so it's one concept. Prerequisites range from none to a first programming course; we conservatively require CS1.

concepts.name: Cell and Molecular Biology (Majors)
concepts.note: First course of the majors biology sequence (cell and molecular). No chemistry prerequisite encoded — most colleges treat General Chemistry as a recommended co-req, not a completed requirement. The integrated lab satisfies the UC's intro-bio-lab ask.
example_cc_titles: Principles of Biology - Molecular and Cellular

concepts.name: Organismal, Evolutionary and Ecological Biology (Majors)
concepts.note: Second majors-biology course — one concept covering organismal, evolution, ecology, and biodiversity however a college packages it (a single course, a botany+zoology pair, or a three-course split). We require cell and molecular biology first, taking the stricter cell-first order.

concepts.name: Human Physiology
concepts.note: A dedicated Human Physiology course (majors / pre-professional). We require both cell and molecular biology and General Chemistry 1 — the conservative call. Multi-semester anatomy and physiology sequences do not map here.

concepts.name: Introductory Circuit Analysis for Engineers
concepts.note: The main engineering transfer course statewide (~50 colleges): intro circuit analysis. Requires calculus-based physics (E&M) plus differential equations — either the standalone Diff Eq course, or a combined Linear Algebra + Diff Eq course. The combined course is the shorter route (one course instead of linear algebra then diff eq), so students who take it reach circuits with less prerequisite depth. Lecture+lab pairs and titles like 'Network Analysis' fold in.

concepts.name: Programming and Computational Methods for Engineers
concepts.note: Programming and computational methods for engineers — covers both the MATLAB flavor and the C/C++/Python-for-engineers flavor. We require Calc 1 completed. A course that's really just a generic CS1 under an ENGR prefix maps to CS1 instead.
example_cc_titles: Computational Methods for Engineers and Scientists
Introduction to Programming Concepts and Methodologies for Engineers

concepts.name: English Composition 1 (Academic Reading & Writing)
concepts.note: First-semester college composition — the most standardized course in the state (~110 colleges). Honors, stretch/extended-support, and multilingual/ESL versions all count. No transfer-level prerequisite (placement is pre-transfer).
example_cc_titles: ENGL C1000 Academic Reading and Writing
ENGL C1000E Academic Reading and Writing (extended)
College Reading and Composition I Plus Lab
ESL 110 College Composition for Non-Native Speakers

concepts.name: English Composition 2 (Critical Thinking & Composition)
concepts.note: Second-semester composition / critical thinking and writing. Includes literature-based versions and philosophy 'Critical Thinking and Writing' courses, since they fill the same slot. Pure logic courses with no writing component stay unmapped.
example_cc_titles: ENGL C1001 Critical Thinking and Writing
Critical Thinking and Writing about Literature
Argumentative Writing and Critical Thinking Through Literature

concepts.name: Public Speaking (Oral Communication)
concepts.note: First-course public speaking / oral communication (~110 colleges). Argumentation, debate, persuasion, and advanced public speaking do not map here.
example_cc_titles: COMM C1000 Introduction to Public Speaking

concepts.name: Principles of Microeconomics
concepts.note: Principles of Microeconomics (~110 colleges). No ordering edge between micro and macro — colleges disagree on which comes first, so forcing one would be wrong. No math gate (the usual algebra prerequisite is pre-transfer).

concepts.name: Principles of Macroeconomics
concepts.note: Principles of Macroeconomics (~110 colleges). Same as micro: no micro/macro ordering and no math gate. One-course 'Intro to Economics' surveys stay unmapped.

concepts.name: Financial Accounting
concepts.note: Financial Accounting — the biggest single cluster (~120 colleges). Managerial accounting isn't in our data, and vocational bookkeeping stays unmapped.

concepts.name: Introduction to Literature
concepts.note: Introduction to Literature and genre-survey courses (~40 colleges). Requires first-semester composition, which nearly every college requires. Composition-2-through-literature courses map to the comp-2 concept instead.

concepts.name: World Literature I (Antiquity to ~1650)
concepts.note: World Literature I (antiquity to ~1650), ~30 colleges. No ordering edge to World Lit II — the two halves are parallel, not sequential, at almost every college. Requires first-semester composition (the gate varies a little; we take the conservative call).
example_cc_titles: Masterpieces of World Literature I: 1500 BCE - 1600 CE
Survey of World Literature to the Mid-Seventeenth Century

concepts.name: World Literature II (~1650 to Present)
concepts.note: World Literature II (~1650 to present), ~30 colleges. Contemporary-world-literature versions fold in here. Requires first-semester composition (conservative call, same as the other literature concepts).
example_cc_titles: Masterpieces of World Literature II: 1600 - Present
World Literature II: Seventeenth Century Through the Present

concepts.name: Linear Algebra + Differential Equations (combined)
concepts.note: For schools that teach Linear Algebra and Differential Equations as one combined course (~20 colleges, e.g. MATH 216 at College of Marin). It requires Calc 3, and its 'satisfies' link lets the single course stand in for both Linear Algebra and Differential Equations wherever those are needed downstream.
example_cc_titles: Introduction to Linear Algebra and Differential Equations
